Introduction
Welcome to Module Five.
This is probably the module that I'm most excited about. This one is all about you
creating a great prelaunch to go into your launch.
This is what makes your launch rock and we're focused on the internal launch here but
frankly all the stuff you're going to learn works for JV launches as well. In fact, you take
this work that you do on your internal launch and you can generally reuse it for your JV
launch. Everything we do here, watch the way I break this down because it's the same
psychology, it's the same messaging that you want to have in your launches.
The way this is going to work, I'm going to give you real world examples from
successful launches.
These are real world launches that actually worked and worked really well. I'm going to
walk you through some of the pieces of prelaunch content and I'm going to break down
some videos and will pause to tell you what's working and what's not working in the
video. We're going to walk you through this piece of the prelaunch content, show you
why they were successful as well as some things that could probably be improved upon
because no one's launch is ever perfect.
!
One thing you'll notice is these are different markets and they're very different
presentation styles because everyone's different. There isn't one straight check that you
have to fit into and their videos, you're going to see they play to their different
strengths.
!
One thing that I don't want to hear from you is "Well, that works for so and so because
they have this," or “They have this kind of position so they could say this, they could
say that or I can't do what she did because it won't work for me for this reason or that
reason. I don't have this resource, I don't have that asset, I don't have this authority.”
!
You have to work with what you've got and you build from there. No one starts off with
every single asset, they started off with nothing in their market and they built it from
there. No one is born as a recognized expert. No one is born with a list. I didn't come
out of the blue with a list. No one is born with a product and each person, they built
their product, they built their position even built their authority. You just keep on taking
those baby steps and you'll get there. That's the way this business works. You just take
one little step and you gradually build authority, then gradually build position and you
move forward from there.
!
!
!
!

PLC Overview
!
Now I'm going to talk about prelaunch content. We're focused specifically on the
internal launch and everything we cover here can go for the JV launch. It's put together
the same way.
!
Module four we talked about the prelaunch and then later on we're going to be talking
about the open cart where we are actually selling stuff but this is the prelaunch. Now,
this is basically that sideway sale letter that we talked about in module one.
In here, you're going to have three pieces of prelaunch content. We've got prelaunch
content one, two, and three and these days in general they're done via video. They
don't have to be done via video but usually they are these days. The examples that I'm
going to be giving you are video based. The reality is that these days video is easy to
do even if you aren't comfortable on camera, you don't have to be on camera, you can
see screen cap video, no problem. You've seen how I've used some video of me being
in front of the camera but also playing screen cap video. You can do it that way if you
don't want to be in front of the camera.
It's generally easier to be more authentic and more engaging on video than it is in
writing. There are so many of us that once we start writing we think you have to sound
like a college professor or something. In any case, video is what's working these days
so we're going to focus on video here.
!
I'm going to walk you through these examples and you're going to see, we do the
sideway sales letter and that problem solution path, that's P as path.
!
That's where you come out and you define a problem and then you give the solution
and then inherent in that solution there's often another problem. Then you give the
solution for that one in your second video and then inherent in that there's usually a
third problem here. Then you solve that problem as well but then there's always
another problem and that's going to get solved in your sales letter, your sales video.
That's the problem solution path.
!
You can also look at it as the opportunity solution where you're showing opportunity
and then you give the solution or how to get there opportunity and then that often
develops into another opportunity and you saw that one as well and you move through
giving opportunities and solutions all the way through.
Very similar as the problem solution path. One example I'm going to give you is Will
Hamilton who teaches tennis instructions. The opportunity is what he's really going to
teach and you could look at it as a problem or as an opportunity.
!

It's like a glass that's half full, that's half empty, what Will is teaching is a course on
tennis strategy. The opportunity is you don't have to spend hours and hours in the
weight room or hours on the court to improve your game. If you sit there and watch
the videos and learn about strategy you can improve your game that way as well.
!
He delivers that big time opportunity and then he talks strategy and defines another
opportunity or sort of a problem within that and then another problem. Eventually it's
like, okay I've taught you all these strategies but then how you actually use those once
you get into a match and you have to react to what the other person is doing is the
next step. You could see how he masterfully does this presenting opportunities or
problems all the way through leading into the sale.
!
There's another thing we talked about, mental triggers, back in module two. We went
through all the mental triggers like authority, likeability, reciprocity, scarcity, community,
social proof, anticipation, interaction. You're going to see as you go through these
examples how these are built in.
!
Often like in this very first piece you have to develop some real authority, some real
positioning for yourself. You have to position yourself as someone that the viewer
should pay attention to. You do that right away or else they're not even going to watch
your video.
You're going to see there's a lot of different ways to create that authority. Going back to
that sideway sales letter, the first video is generally about defining an opportunity for
some type of transformation, some type of change, some type of result.
!
Whether they're going to lose weight, find the love of their life, make more money, cut
some strokes off their golf score, or learn how to bake an amazing cake. There is some
type of opportunity where you really start to make that transformation real for them.
Show them how the transformation can be absolutely real and that second video is
often the most flexible. Frankly it's often the weakest video. Generally, you want to
open really strong with a really strong video. You want to close really strong and if
you're going to have a weak one, it's going to be the middle one but that's a hidden
secret. Not every video is perfect or fantastic or awesome. If I look at my at my launch
videos, I'll often say, "Well that one was really great. That one was okay." We kept our
head above water and then we killed it with this one.
Frankly, a lot of times it's that second video that doesn't completely crush it. I want you
to crush every single one of your videos, I hope you do. I'm going to do my best to
teach you how to do that but reality is reality. We don't all hit a home run every single
time. You're going to see that as we go through these videos. I'm going to point out the
good and the bad. Remember there is flexibility in here.

Over-The-Shoulder Real Life Example: The Tennis Ninja Launch
!
Now, I want to walk you through an example here. This is Will Hamilton of
FuzzyYellowBalls.com. That's Will's business, it's a site about tennis instruction and this
is his Tennis Ninja launch. That's the name of the product, Tennis Ninja.
!
We're going to look at his first piece of prelaunch content, his first video. We're going to
walk step by step through this. I'm going to show you exactly what he's doing and
some details about his launch. He has a 17,000 person launch list for this launch. He did
$168,323 in sales. It was a great, great launch. Will did this launch less than a year
after he joined Product Launch Formula.
For disclosure, Will is one of my Platinum Plus members now, but he did this not even a
year after he joined Product Launch Formula. His launch has gone from $35,000 to
$65,000 to $105,000. This launch, he did $168,000 and it had built up to his next
launch that did $400,000. You can see that progression through his launch results. This
is an example of the launch echo. The launch echo is this idea of all the extra benefits
that come to you from doing a launch besides just making the sales. A lot of times
people talk about the money and here I am talking about the money.

This is the deal. There are certain triggers that create enormous influence in us, in our
brains, that cause us to make decisions, that really cause us to take actions. These
mental triggers exist in every one of us and they have been with us as humans for
thousands of years, and it’s very likely they’ll be with us for thousands of years more.
No matter who you are, where you live in the world, these mental triggers definitely
have an impact on you and your decision making – and me and my decision making.
We’re all susceptible to these triggers.
Let me just go through a few of them. I actually have 20 of these that I’ve identified,
but I don’t have time to go through all 20 in this video. Let’s just go through a few of
the top ones.
First of all is community. Community is a very powerful trigger. It’s like if we perceive
ourselves as being part of a community we will act in accordance to how we think the
people in that community are supposed to act. Really powerful
Another one is anticipation. This is the idea where we anticipate something happening,
anticipating events. If you think about how powerful events are, like birthdays,
graduations, holidays, and how we look forward to those. Think about the way TV is,
almost any TV shows they have these cliffhangers at the end of the show that leave you
anticipating and pull you in for the next show. Even the most basic sitcoms use
anticipation.
Then there’s authority. Authority is an incredibly powerful trigger and it’s a great one to
use in your launches. The launch process really brings out the authority. It’s one of the
most powerful. That’s basically when we perceive someone as being an authority, we
listen to them. They are very influential. Almost any type of uniform, like a police officer
or a doctor, that creates authority and it’s very powerful.
Reciprocity is another great trigger. That’s the idea of it we give something to someone,
they’re going to feel an obligation to give something back to us. And it works the other
way around. If you give a gift to someone, they’re going to feel like they want to give a
gift back to you. If someone gives you a gift, you’re going to feel like you need to give
them a gift back.
Then there’s social proof. This is the idea where if we see a lot of other people doing
something then we’re going to think, “Maybe there’s something there,” and we’re going
to be more inclined to do that as well.
Finally, there’s scarcity. If there is less of something, then we want it more. Gold,
diamonds, Ferraris, whatever, just the fact that there’s fewer of something instantly
raises that value and gets us more interested.

Those triggers are everywhere in our lives. They work everywhere in our lives. But I
want to talk about how you can take these and layer them into your launch.
Here we have our sideways sales letter, we have our P.S. path there where we’re
providing great value, we’re defining their problems, giving them solutions to their
problems, building this relationship. One of the things is right off the bat when we give
some great value – and what I want you to do in your launch is give great value, give
some of your best content away in that very first piece – you instantly create this
authority.
They’re like, “Holy cow.” First of all, you’re just demonstrating how much you know,
how comfortable you are, and that just instantly builds authority. There’s this idea that
you can just give this stuff away for free, that’s massive authority.
Then right off the bat what you’re always doing is underneath each one of these you’re
building it in a way to get feedback. If you’re putting this video out, you have
comments down below the video. When there are comments down there and people
can see other people are watching your video and leaving comments, that builds up
social proof. That’s the idea of, “All these other people are watching this, there must be
something there and there must be something going on.”
Then as you move through here, you actually start to build up community. The
community actually develops down in those comments. You have comments down
below each one of these videos, people will start answering it, you can start answering
them. It also brings in another trigger that I didn’t mention, which is conversation.
People love a conversation. They would much rather be in a conversation than be
listening to a monologue.
Of course, you’re also building reciprocity because you’re giving great content. As you
give great content, people are loving you and they want to give something back to you.
Then as you start to move through here and get deeper, you just keep on building
social proof, you keep on building community, but now you start to build anticipation.
People are looking forward to your final offer. You’re taking your marketing and you’re
turning into an event.
All through here you’re building this authority, now you’re starting to build anticipation.
Then also you want to build in some scarcity. As you get closer to the end, you

Literally, that will double the sales in your launch if you have that hard deadline at the

The reality is it’s a lot easier to sell to someone if they know, like, and trust you. I like
to think of it in terms of this B.S. detector. It’s like you walk onto a car lot, you see the
door open up and the salesperson is coming towards you and instantly your B.S.
detector is in the red zone. You’re not going to trust them – or maybe you trust them,
but you’re not going to trust them as well as you trust your best friend. On the other
hand, if your best friend says, “Hey, I just bought this new car. I love this new car. You
have to check it out, it’s completely awesome.” Your B.S. detector is backed way off,
you trust them, you believe them. Maybe you know they tend to have a different
opinion on things, but you definitely trust them.
The thing is that old style marketing, that old style sales letter, or even those broken
launches where you don’t provide any value, that B.S. detector is in the red zone. But if
you do this, if you show people their problem-solution-problem-solution, if you layer in
all these mental triggers, that B.S. detector backs way off into the safe zone.
Then when you actually do get to your sales letter or sales video it might spike up a
little bit, because you’re eventually going to ask for some money, you have to do that to
make the sale, but the reality is that B.S. detector stays a lot further down in the safe
zone because you’ve built up that relationship. What we’re really talking about is
creating a conversation with your market, delivering real value, creating a real
relationship and then asking for the sale.

wanted to be able to create a product basically in partnership with my clients and I

The seed launch is great if you have no list and no product. You want to think about it
like you need to have a small list, 50 to 100 people would be great. These days it’s easy
to get that in social media. If you just start publishing about something on Facebook, a
couple times a week publish about whatever topic you want to start your business in,
you can attract followers very quickly. Go out and take part in the social media
discussions. It’s very easy to build those. Go crazy, get 200 or 300 people on the list.
That would be even better. I’ve seen people do this starting with 30 or 40 people.
This is how the seed launch looks. Think of these dots as maybe 10 people per dot or
so. I don’t know how many dots I have there, but let’s just say it’s 12 of them and we
have 120 people on the list. The thing about really small lists like this is they tend to be
very responsive. Small lists are extremely responsive because of this concept of
recency, the other one is that they know you. They really feel like they know you, you
feel like you know them, and you probably have interactions with them. So small lists
can be very responsive.
You put together a very simple sideways sales letter. Basically just a couple pieces of
prelaunch content, as opposed to over here with the internal launch where you really
do the classic three pieces of prelaunch content then the sales letter, here you could do
one or two pieces of prelaunch.
One of the things I want to mention is the seed launch is great if you’re just starting
out. It’s absolutely great for that. It’s also great if you have a current business, but you
have a new offer you want to come out with, you want help creating that product, you
want to test out an offer. I still use seed launches to this day. I did one just a few
months ago, it was so much fun and I loved doing it.
You put out a couple of small pieces of prelaunch content and then the way you make
your offer is that you’re actually going to deliver it via a series of calls. Let’s say you
were going to teach guitar.
The message in the prelaunch could be, “People have been asking me, I’ve been
publishing on my blog, I’ve been publishing on Facebook with tips about how to learn
guitar, people keep on asking me if I would just go ahead and teach it. So what I’ve
decided to is put together a series of teleseminars,” – or webinars, you can pick
whichever one you want.
You put together a series of teleseminars and then you deliver your content over three
teleseminars. You could do five if you have a more in-depth topic and you need five
calls to cover it, but you could do just three calls. What you do is make this offer,
generally what you’re looking for is it would be ideal to get 20 or 30 people in your
program. I’ll explain why.
After they go through your mini sideways sales letter and then buy from you, the first
thing you do is send them to a survey. In the survey you’re asking them what they need
Copyright © All Rights Reserved Internet Alchemy, Inc. 2014 -2015 	Page ! 74
PLF_Module5_complete_v2.0
GROUPINSIDERS.COM EXCLUSIVE

-- 74 of 94 --

to know about the topic. It could be just like, “I’m really excited about this program. I’m
getting ready to start teaching it. I just need to make sure that I cover this topic
completely. Could you do me a favor and just tell me if you’re going to sit down with me
for lunch or for coffee, what would be the top two questions you would ask me?” You
send your people through that survey, those questions tell you what you need to cover
in your product.
After that first survey you could just take a look at it, take all the questions that
everyone sent in and group them into three main topics. Those three topics are your
calls. Then you do your first call and you’re basically getting on there and answering the
questions people have asked you.
Then after that call you run them through another survey that basically says, “That was
a great call. I loved teaching that, loved being on the call with you. Great interaction. I
just have a couple of questions for you. Is there anything that isn’t 100% clear about
what I covered in that last call?” That’s the first question. The second question is, “Our
next call is going to be about ______. What are your topic two questions about that?”
Then that tells you what you need to cover in that next call. Then you just keep on
doing that; survey, call, survey, call, for as many calls as you want to do.
One of the things that will make all the difference for your business is that you always
want to over deliver. This is something I absolutely obsess about. It’s really easy in the
seed launch to over deliver by just doing a bonus call that you don’t announce up front.
You don’t announce it until you’re somewhere between the first and the second call.
You just say, “Hey, by the way, I told you that you’re going to get three calls, but we’re
actually going to have bonus call. The bonus call is going to be the best call of them all
and you’re going to love it.”
Then after your final regular call, the call you promised, then you send them to another
survey and say, “I just want to know if there’s any questions whatsoever. We’re going to
have a wide open Q&A on this next call,” and then you send them into the bonus call.
The whole entire process there’s so much interaction, because there are so many
surveys, and most of the calls you can actually take live questions. I love taking live
questions on my call. Because you’re doing that all the way through, you are going to
create a great product. A lot of people worry about creating products and they worry
that their product isn’t going to be good enough or that it’s not going to be perfect.
This product is tuned to the market, because every step of the way you’re asking
people what they need to know. It just creates the best product you ever could. You
end up with very happy clients. You generally end up with great testimonials. You
usually end up with great case studies.
And then because you’re recording these calls all the way through, you end up with the
recordings. If you did three calls and they each lasted an hour to 90 minutes, you’re
going to end up with four or five hours of audio. You can take that and get it
transcribed. Those four or five hours of audio are probably going to be 60 – 80 – maybe
even 90 pages of content. Now you have an audio product, you have a book, and

I’ve got these numbered one, two, and three. The typical way you’ll do this is you’ll
start off with a seed launch, then you’ll build up to an internal launch, and then you’ll
go to a JV launch. I did it a little backwards. Since I was inventing this in the first place,

This second business I skipped, I did a seed launch and then I went straight into a JV
launch, because I had no list whatsoever in this market, but I did know some JV
partners. JV stands for joint venture. Think of it like an affiliate. Someone else who has
a list of subscribers and they will mail their list and tell them about your product. In
exchange, if you make sales to the people they send into your launch then you will give
them a commission.
This is what a JV launch looks like. It’s basically the same sideways sales letter as you
did up there, but here you’re having your joint venture partners who already have the
lists and they’re mailing into your launch. Up here you have a sideways sales letter,
three pieces of prelaunch content – three pieces of prelaunch content down here. Often
it could be the same piece of prelaunch content.
In other words, often you could do an internal launch, put together your sideways sales
letter, you prelaunch content, and then after that internal launch you could do a JV
launch after you’ve figured out your numbers, your metrics, your offer, your systems,
then you can go recruit joint venture partners and do the sideways sales letter down
here. The big difference is you’re just getting these partners who have lists to mail into
your sideways sales letter.
The great thing about a JV launch is they can be so highly leveraged. Think about up
here, even at the end when I had 80,000 subscribers, I had 80,000 but what if you got
three or four of five joint venture partners and each one of them had 50,000
subscribers. You can see how these can be highly leveraged and take your results to a
completely different level. That’s what joint venture launches do.
I was coming out of here, I knew I had a great product that I had created with the

the selling, and about what could really make a difference in people’s lives with this
product, and then I went out and recruited some joint venture partners and did a joint
venture launch.
I got a lot of great support from these joint venture partners. Again, I had started with

The thing with that is when you have joint venture partners you end up paying them

Three types of launches; the seed launch, the internal launch, the JV launch. Typically
when you’re starting out you’ll start out with the seed launch, then go to an internal
launch, and go to a JV launch. But enormously flexible, you saw I sort of jumped

First, you’ve got an existing business and you’re looking to build that.
Second, you might not have an online business, you might be some type of practitioner,

If you’ve got that great business already, then you are just one internal launch away

potential JV partners and you could do a JV launch, that’s where you really leverage up.
If you’re in that second category and you’re a practitioner of some type, maybe you

If you’re absolutely just starting from scratch, then it’s all about the seed launch for

Product Launch Formula PLC 3
By now you know that you need to launch. You know that every successful business,
every successful brand, every successful product starts with a successful product
launch. You do not want to show up slowly. You don’t want to depend on hope
marketing. That’s where you put all this time into creating your product, creating your
business and then you hope it does well. That doesn’t work so well. You need to
engineer your success. You need to properly execute a product launch to ensure your
success. This video is all about blueprinting that launch in creating your product launch
path.
I'm Jeff Walker and since 2005 I've been training entrepreneurs how to launch their
